query: effectiveness of zeolite hemostat in managing arterial injuries

retrieved_docs.title: Hemorrhage Control -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: In the early 2000s, hemostatic agents were being pressed into development. A chitosan-based dressing and a zeolite mineral powder were the first approved by the US military. [15] These hemostatic agents were most useful for non-compressible wounds. Chitosan-based dressings worked by adhering to hemorrhaging tissues and sealing vessels; however, their efficacy diminished over time. The zeolite mineral powder's mechanism of action is based on rapid water absorption, concentrating clotting factors and cells and increase clot formation. A dry fibrin sealant dressing was developed using concentrated fibrinogen and thrombin from donated human plasma. However, its expense and increased testing requirements prevented its widespread use. Similarly, a chitin-based dressing was created, but its cost far exceeds other equally effective agents.

retrieved_docs.title: Hemorrhage Control -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: One product, made of smectite minerals, was developed to both concentrate clotting elements by desiccation and activate the intrinsic coagulation pathway. While the products made of smectite minerals proved to be highly effective at hemostasis, studies showed that it caused thrombi to form that could migrate throughout the circulatory system, resulting in devastating complications. A surgical gauze impregnated with kaolin and aluminum silicate also proved to be effective at activating the intrinsic pathway and facilitating hemostasis without known side effects. Following these studies, the CoTCCC decided in 2015 to recommend surgical gauze impregnated with kaolin and aluminum silicate as the "hemostatic agent of choice." [12] Two chitosan-based dressings were recommended as alternatives to the surgical gauze impregnated with kaolin and aluminum silicate. Lastly, a syringe that injects expandable sponges into the wound tract to create a physical barrier and pressure inside the cavity proved highly effective against narrow but deep penetrating wounds in some regions.

retrieved_docs.title: Mohs Micrographic Surgery: Anticoagulants and Hemostatic Agents Impact -- Other Issues -- Caustic Agents
retrieved_docs.content: Applying topical caustic hemostatic agents induces hemostasis by inducing tissue necrosis and protein coagulation, leading to thrombus formation in the exposed cutaneous vessels. [29] Topical caustic hemostatic agents include ferric subsulfate, aluminum chloride, and zinc paste, commonly used in superficial wounds created by shave or punch biopsies. Aluminum chloride is the most frequently used agent in this class due to its low cost, widespread availability, and minimal adverse effect profile characterized by mild pain and tissue irritation. [29] Ferric subsulfate, compounded as Monsel solution, is equally effective but has fallen out of favor somewhat due to the risk of permanent dyspigmentation from dermal iron placement. [30] The use of zinc paste, primarily composed of zinc chloride, was described by Frederic F. Mohs in the chemical fixation of skin cancer. However, despite its anti-tumor properties, it is now infrequently used in the context of MMS. [29]

retrieved_docs.title: Splenic Trauma -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Embolization is achieved via coils, plugs, or gelatin sponges. Coils are detachable or pushable; newer coil versions may come with attached nylon or hydrogel to enhance thrombus formation. Gelatin sponges (eg, Gelfoam) are made from porcine skin, gelatin, and water. These sponges are highly absorptive and serve as a temporary embolic agent, typically reabsorbing within several weeks. [19] The benefits of gelatin sponges used in conjunction with distal embolization are potentially greater splenic salvage, preservation of proximal access points, and reduced cost. [25] [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Splenic Trauma -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Proximal embolization is associated with less contrast and shorter procedure time. Distal embolization, however, requires microcatheters, more contrast, and takes longer to complete. There is ongoing debate regarding the comparative risks and benefits of the optimal technique, equipment, and various methods. Topics of discussion include rebleeding rates, infection, infarction, and the efficacy of coils and plugs versus foam embolic agents. [22] [23] [24] Vascular access for embolization is obtained through either the radial or femoral artery. Radial artery access may result in quicker splenic artery cannulation. Once access is established, the celiac artery is cannulated, and an angiogram is obtained to visualize the blood supply. The goal is to preserve perfusion to the pancreas and allow collaterals to reach the spleen.

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Junctional Hemorrhage Control -- Technique or Treatment -- Hemostatic Dressings
retrieved_docs.content: QuikClot Combat Gauze: The CoTCCC hemostatic dressing of choice. This kaolin-impregnated polyester-rayon dressing functions both as a factor concentrator and procoagulant. [11] Kaolin is an inorganic mineral and activator of the intrinsic clotting pathway, expediting clot formation. [17] [18] It is the most widely studied hemostatic dressing and is known to have a good safety profile. [15] An Israel Defense Forces Medical Corps study involving 37 junctional hemorrhage cases found that QuikClot Combat gauze produced hemostasis in 88.6% of cases. [19] Combat gauze is currently the primary hemostatic dressing used by all US Operational Forces and NATO military organizations. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Mohs Micrographic Surgery Surgical Complication Management -- Issues of Concern -- Bleeding and Hematoma Formation
retrieved_docs.content: In MMS, various hemostatic methods, including manual pressure, electrosurgery, heat cautery, topical hemostatics, and vessel ligation, are employed to manage bleeding from ruptured vessels during the incision, extirpation, and undermining processes. [25] For superficial or dermal oozing, topical caustic agents, electrosurgery, heat cautery, and manual pressure are typically utilized. [25] [26] Topical caustic agents coagulate proteins, leading to tissue necrosis and eschar formation. They are typically applied via a cotton tip applicator to a dried wound. [26] Aluminum chloride is most commonly used, but other agents include 20% ferric subsulfate, silver nitrate, and zinc chloride. [26] Noncaustic topical hemostatics, physical or physiological agents, can be applied for deeper oozing wounds or postsurgical preventative measures. [26] Physical agents, including porcine gelatins, microporous polysaccharide hemispheres, hydrophilic polymers with potassium salts, cellulose, and collagen compounds, provide a scaffold for platelet aggregation. Physiological agents such as fibrin, thrombin, and platelet gels promote hemostasis by enhancing the augmentation and propagation phases. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Vascular Extremity Trauma -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: If an intimal defect is present on imaging, no invasive intervention is necessary as 87% to 95% will heal without vascular operative intervention. The patient can be placed on heparin or aspirin for prophylaxis as long as there is no contraindication. Urgent imaging is necessary if there is an abrupt change in the flow to an extremity or signs of AV fistula or pseudoaneurysm. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Vascular Extremity Trauma -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: After repair of a peripheral artery a completion arteriogram should be done in a stable patient if there was a delay in diagnosis and/or treatment, complex repair done (segmental resection and an end-to-end anastomosis or insertion of an interposition graft), if palpable pulses are not present distally after repair or there is concern for narrowing at anastomosis. Metal clips can be placed at the distal and proximal end of the repair to aid with the arteriogram.  If the anastomosis appears narrowed and the patient is stable, cut out the polypropylene suture, debride any questionable tissue (especially adventitia) and if the artery appears to be in vasospasm, a dilator (Garrett coronary artery or Bakes biliary) can be inserted. It is recommended to use an interrupted suture technique if a continuous technique was used the first time. [17]

retrieved_docs.content: While hemostatic agents can benefit hemostasis in anticoagulated patients, no single agent suits every clinical circumstance. Topical hemostatic agents used in cutaneous surgery include caustic, noncaustic, and physiologic agents, each with a different mechanism of action.

retrieved_docs.title: Kidney Trauma -- Treatment / Management -- Angiographic Embolization
retrieved_docs.content: Selective angiography with angioembolization has revolutionized the evaluation and management of the bleeding patient from a renal injury with a successful embolization rate as high as 88% and renal salvage rate up to 92%. [7] [3] An interventional radiologist is needed for this procedure and can be only done at the institutions where they can be available immediately. If this service is not available then the patient should be transferred if it's stable to do so. Angioembolization is preferred over surgery as it has been shown to reduce renal loss and preserve renal function and so increase renal salvage overall. [13] [7] [3] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2545
retrieved_docs.content: Injection or infusion of other therapeutic or prophylactic substance is linked to a range of vascular interventional techniques. Open peripheral arterial or venous catheterization with administration of thrombolytic or chemotherapeutic agents represents a surgical approach for delivering these agents directly into the arterial or venous system. Percutaneous peripheral arterial or venous catheterization with administration of thrombolytic or chemotherapeutic agents by pulse spray technique provides a minimally invasive method that uses a spray of the therapeutic substance to achieve rapid distribution. Percutaneous peripheral arterial or venous catheterization with administration of thrombolytic or chemotherapeutic agents by continuous infusion offers another minimally invasive option, delivering the agents steadily over time to maintain therapeutic levels. Transcatheter embolization of blood vessel is an additional catheter‑based procedure in which therapeutic or prophylactic substances are used to occlude a vessel, often to control bleeding or to treat vascular lesions. Together, these procedures illustrate the diverse ways that injection or infusion of other therapeutic or prophylactic substances can be administered through open, percutaneous, or transcatheter vascular access methods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 2313
retrieved_docs.content: Injection or infusion of other therapeutic or prophylactic substance is linked to several interventional techniques for delivering agents such as thrombolytics or chemotherapeutics. It can be performed through open peripheral arterial or venous catheterization with administration of thrombolytic or chemotherapeutic agents, through percutaneous peripheral arterial or venous catheterization using a pulse‑spray technique for the same types of agents, and via transcatheter embolization of a blood vessel. These procedural approaches illustrate the various catheter‑based methods by which therapeutic or prophylactic substances may be introduced into the vascular system to achieve targeted treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2235
retrieved_docs.content: Injection or infusion of other therapeutic or prophylactic substance can be administered through several catheter‑based techniques. One approach involves open peripheral arterial or venous catheterization with the administration of thrombolytic or chemotherapeutic agents, allowing direct delivery of these drugs into the vascular system. A less invasive alternative is percutaneous peripheral arterial or venous catheterization with continuous infusion of thrombolytic or chemotherapeutic agents, which provides sustained drug exposure while minimizing surgical exposure. Additionally, transcatheter embolization of a blood vessel can be used to deliver therapeutic or prophylactic substances by occluding targeted vessels, thereby concentrating the agent at the desired site. Together, these methods illustrate the range of procedural options for delivering therapeutic and prophylactic substances via arterial or venous access.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2449
retrieved_docs.content: The Vascular System is identified as the anatomical site linked to both اضطراب الأوعية الكلوية (renal vascular disorder) and تسلخ الشرايين (arterial erosion). These conditions involve pathological changes within the blood vessels, with اضطراب الأوعية الكلوية affecting the renal vasculature and تسلخ الشرايين involving damage to arterial walls. Recognizing that both disorders share the Vascular System as their underlying anatomic substrate underscores the central role of vascular health in the development and management of renal vascular disease and arterial erosion, and highlights the need for comprehensive vascular assessment and care in patients presenting with these conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 891
retrieved_docs.content: Heparin, an anticoagulant medication, may be employed in the management of احتشاء دماغي (cerebral infarction or stroke). By inhibiting clot formation, heparin can help prevent the propagation of thrombi in patients experiencing this type of brain injury, underscoring its therapeutic role in acute stroke care.
